input: What government position was held by the woman who portrayed Corliss Archer in the film Kiss and Tell?

task.prompt: What government position was held by the woman who portrayed Corliss Archer in the film Kiss and Tell?
task.context:
[1] Meet Corliss Archer
Meet Corliss Archer, a program from radio's Golden Age, ran from January 7, 1943 to September 30, 1956. Although it was CBS's answer to NBC's popular "A Date with Judy", it was also broadcast by NBC in 1948 as a summer replacement for "The Bob Hope Show". From October 3, 1952 to June 26, 1953, it aired on ABC, finally returning to CBS. Despite the program's long run, fewer than 24 episodes are known to exist.

[2] Shirley Temple
Shirley Temple Black (April 23, 1928 – February 10, 2014) was an American actress, singer, dancer, businesswoman, and diplomat who was Hollywood's number one box-office draw as a child actress from 1935 to 1938. As an adult, she was named United States ambassador to Ghana and to Czechoslovakia and also served as Chief of Protocol of the United States.

[3] Janet Waldo
Janet Marie Waldo (February 4, 1920 – June 12, 2016) was an American radio and voice actress. She is best known in animation for voicing Judy Jetson, Nancy in "Shazzan", Penelope Pitstop, and Josie in "Josie and the Pussycats", and on radio as the title character in "Meet Corliss Archer".

[4] Meet Corliss Archer (TV series)
Meet Corliss Archer is an American television sitcom that aired on CBS (July 13, 1951 - August 10, 1951) and in syndication via the Ziv Company from April to December 1954. The program was an adaptation of the radio series of the same name, which was based on a series of short stories by F. Hugh Herbert.

[5] Lord High Treasurer
The post of Lord High Treasurer or Lord Treasurer was an English government position and has been a British government position since the Acts of Union of 1707. A holder of the post would be the third-highest-ranked Great Officer of State, below the Lord High Steward and the Lord High Chancellor.

[6] A Kiss for Corliss
A Kiss for Corliss is a 1949 American comedy film directed by Richard Wallace and written by Howard Dimsdale. It stars Shirley Temple in her final starring role as well as her final film appearance. It is a sequel to the 1945 film "Kiss and Tell". "A Kiss for Corliss" was retitled "Almost a Bride" before release and this title appears in the title sequence. The film was released on November 25, 1949, by United Artists.

[7] Kiss and Tell (1945 film)
Kiss and Tell is a 1945 American comedy film starring then 17-year-old Shirley Temple as Corliss Archer. In the film, two teenage girls cause their respective parents much concern when they start to become interested in boys. The parents' bickering about which girl is the worse influence causes more problems than it solves.

[8] Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs
The office of Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs was a British Government position, created in 2003. Certain functions of the Lord Chancellor which related to the Lord Chancellor's Department were transferred to the Secretary of State. At a later date further functions were also transferred to the Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs from the First Secretary of State, a position within the government held by the Deputy Prime Minister.

[9] Village accountant
The Village Accountant (variously known as "Patwari", "Talati", "Patel", "Karnam", "Adhikari", "Shanbogaru","Patnaik" etc.) is an administrative government position found in rural parts of the Indian sub-continent. The office and the officeholder are called the "patwari" in Telangana, Bengal, North India and in Pakistan while in Sindh it is called "tapedar". The position is known as the "karnam" in Andhra Pradesh, "patnaik" in Orissa or "adhikari" in Tamil Nadu, while it is commonly known as the "talati" in Karnataka, Gujarat and Maharashtra. The position was known as the "kulkarni" in Northern Karnataka and Maharashtra. The position was known as the "shanbogaru" in South Karnataka.

[10] Charles Craft
Charles Craft (May 9, 1902 – September 19, 1968) was an English-born American film and television editor. Born in the county of Hampshire in England on May 9, 1902, Craft would enter the film industry in Hollywood in 1927. The first film he edited was the Universal Pictures silent film, "Painting the Town". Over the next 25 years, Craft would edit 90 feature-length films. In the early 1950s he would switch his focus to the small screen, his first show being "Racket Squad", from 1951–53, for which he was the main editor, editing 93 of the 98 episodes. He would work on several other series during the 1950s, including "Meet Corliss Archer" (1954), "Science Fiction Theatre" (1955–56), and "Highway Patrol" (1955–57). In the late 1950s and early 1960s he was one of the main editors on "Sea Hunt", starring Lloyd Bridges, editing over half of the episodes. His final film work would be editing "Flipper's New Adventure" (1964, the sequel to 1963's "Flipper". When the film was made into a television series, Craft would begin the editing duties on that show, editing the first 28 episodes before he retired in 1966. Craft died on September 19, 1968 in Los Angeles, California.
task.deliverable: Answer the question using only the provided context. Start with a short answer phrase, then briefly justify it.
evaluation_criteria: Answer should match the gold answer under official normalization.
Reasoning should rely on the provided context.
references: Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs

Meet Corliss Archer is an American television sitcom that aired on CBS (July 13, 1951 - August 10, 1951) and in syndication via the Ziv Company from April to December 1954. The program was an adaptation of the radio series of the same name, which was based on a series of short stories by F. Hugh Herbert.

[5] Lord High Treasurer
The post of Lord High Treasurer or Lord Treasurer was an English government position and has been a British government position since the Acts of Union of 1707. A holder of the post would be the third-highest-ranked Great Officer of State, below the Lord High Steward and the Lord High Chancellor.

[6] A Kiss for Corliss

In the film, two teenage girls cause their respective parents much concern when they start to become interested in boys. The parents' bickering about which girl is the worse influence causes more problems than it solves.

[8] Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs
The office of Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs was a British Government position, created in 2003. Certain functions of the Lord Chancellor which related to the Lord Chancellor's Department were transferred to the Secretary of State. At a later date further functions were also transferred to the Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs from the First Secretary of State, a position within the government held by the Deputy Prime Minister.

[9] Village accountant
The Village Accountant (variously known as "Patwari", "Talati", "Patel", "Karnam", "Adhikari", "Shanbogaru","Patnaik" etc.) is an administrative government position found in rural parts of the Indian sub-continent. The office and the officeholder are called the "patwari" in Telangana, Bengal, North India and in Pakistan while in Sindh it is called "tapedar". The position is known as the "karnam" in Andhra Pradesh, "patnaik" in Orissa or "adhikari" in Tamil Nadu, while it is commonly known as the "talati" in Karnataka, Gujarat and Maharashtra. The position was known as the "kulkarni" in Northern Karnataka and Maharashtra. The position was known as the "shanbogaru" in South Karnataka.

[10] Charles Craft
Charles Craft (May 9, 1902 – September 19, 1968) was an English-born American film and television editor. Born in the county of Hampshire in England on May 9, 1902, Craft would enter the film industry in Hollywood in 1927. The first film he edited was the Universal Pictures silent film, "Painting the Town". Over the next 25 years, Craft would edit 90 feature-length films. In the early 1950s he would switch his focus to the small screen, his first show being "Racket Squad", from 1951–53, for which he was the main editor, editing 93 of the 98 episodes. He would work on several other series during the 1950s, including "Meet Corliss Archer" (1954), "Science Fiction Theatre" (1955–56), and "Highway Patrol" (1955–57). In the late 1950s and early 1960s he was one of the main editors on "Sea Hunt", starring Lloyd Bridges, editing over half of the episodes. His final film work would be editing "Flipper's New Adventure" (1964, the sequel to 1963's "Flipper". When the film was made into a television series, Craft would begin the editing duties on that show, editing the first 28 episodes before he retired in 1966. Craft died on September 19, 1968 in Los Angeles, California.

steps.response:
Problem Restatement:  
Identify the government position held by the individual who portrayed Corliss Archer in the 1945 film *Kiss and Tell*.  

Success Criteria:  
- Correctly identify the actress (Shirley Temple) via film credits.  
- Accurately link Shirley Temple to her government role using contextual evidence.  

Plan:  
1. Determine the actress who played Corliss Archer in *Kiss and Tell* (reference [7] explicitly names Shirley Temple).  
2. Cross-reference Shirley Temple’s career with the provided context to find her government position (reference [2] lists her roles as U.S. ambassador and Chief of Protocol).  
3. Validate that the position is a recognized government role (e.g., U.S. ambassadorship).  

Risks and Unknowns:  
- Ambiguity in distinguishing between Shirley Temple’s roles (e.g., "ambassador to Ghana" vs. "Chief of Protocol").  
- Potential confusion with British government positions (e.g., "Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs").  
- Ensuring the answer aligns with the U.S. context implied by the film’s production.  

Research Brief:  
Focus on references [2] and [7] to confirm Shirley Temple’s portrayal of Corliss Archer and her subsequent government roles. Prioritize evidence of her ambassadorships or diplomatic positions, as these are explicitly tied to her career post-film. Avoid conflating British roles (e.g., reference [8]) with U.S. positions.

steps.response:
Evidence and Observations:  
- Reference [7] explicitly states that Shirley Temple played Corliss Archer in the 1945 film *Kiss and Tell*.  
- Reference [2] confirms Shirley Temple’s roles as United States ambassador to Ghana and Czechoslovakia, and as Chief of Protocol of the United States.  
- Reference [8] mentions the British position of Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs, which is distinct from U.S. roles.  
- Reference [10] notes Charles Craft’s editing work on *Meet Corliss Archer* (1954), but this is unrelated to the actress’s government position.  

Options:  
1. United States ambassador to Ghana and Czechoslovakia.  
2. Chief of Protocol of the United States.  
3. Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs (British position, though referenced in the context).  

Assumptions:  
- The film *Kiss and Tell* (1945) is the correct reference point for identifying the actress, as other mentions of Corliss Archer (e.g., the radio/TV series) are unrelated to the film.  
- Shirley Temple’s government roles are distinct from British positions, and the context explicitly ties her roles to the U.S. government.  
- The question seeks a U.S. government position, not a British one, despite the presence of British references in the context.  

Open Questions:  
- Is there ambiguity in distinguishing between Shirley Temple’s roles as ambassador and Chief of Protocol?  
- Could the term “government position” in the question include non-diplomatic roles (e.g., Chief of Protocol) or strictly foreign service roles (e.g., ambassador)?  
- Are there other U.S. government positions not explicitly mentioned in the context that Shirley Temple might have held?  

Research Handoff:  
Prioritize references [2] and [7] to confirm Shirley Temple’s portrayal of Corliss Archer and her U.S. government roles. Focus on validating the ambassadorship and Chief of Protocol as distinct, recognized positions. Exclude British roles (e.g., reference [8]) unless explicitly tied to U.S. context.

<task_prompt>
What government position was held by the woman who portrayed Corliss Archer in the film Kiss and Tell?
</task_prompt>

<task_domain>
multi_hop_qa
</task_domain>

<benchmark_name>
hotpotqa_distractor
</benchmark_name>

<benchmark_split>
dev
</benchmark_split>

<task_type>
open_qa
</task_type>

<task_context>
[1] Meet Corliss Archer
Meet Corliss Archer, a program from radio's Golden Age, ran from January 7, 1943 to September 30, 1956. Although it was CBS's answer to NBC's popular "A Date with Judy", it was also broadcast by NBC in 1948 as a summer replacement for "The Bob Hope Show". From October 3, 1952 to June 26, 1953, it aired on ABC, finally returning to CBS. Despite the program's long run, fewer than 24 episodes are known to exist.

[2] Shirley Temple
Shirley Temple Black (April 23, 1928 – February 10, 2014) was an American actress, singer, dancer, businesswoman, and diplomat who was Hollywood's number one box-office draw as a child actress from 1935 to 1938. As an adult, she was named United States ambassador to Ghana and to Czechoslovakia and also served as Chief of Protocol of the United States.

[3] Janet Waldo
Janet Marie Waldo (February 4, 1920 – June 12, 2016) was an American radio and voice actress. She is best known in animation for voicing Judy Jetson, Nancy in "Shazzan", Penelope Pitstop, and Josie in "Josie and the Pussycats", and on radio as the title character in "Meet Corliss Archer".

[4] Meet Corliss Archer (TV series)
Meet Corliss Archer is an American television sitcom that aired on CBS (July 13, 1951 - August 10, 1951) and in syndication via the Ziv Company from April to December 1954. The program was an adaptation of the radio series of the same name, which was based on a series of short stories by F. Hugh Herbert.

[5] Lord High Treasurer
The post of Lord High Treasurer or Lord Treasurer was an English government position and has been a British government position since the Acts of Union of 1707. A holder of the post would be the third-highest-ranked Great Officer of State, below the Lord High Steward and the Lord High Chancellor.

[6] A Kiss for Corliss
A Kiss for Corliss is a 1949 American comedy film directed by Richard Wallace and written by Howard Dimsdale. It stars Shirley Temple in her final starring role as well as her final film appearance. It is a sequel to the 1945 film "Kiss and Tell". "A Kiss for Corliss" was retitled "Almost a Bride" before release and this title appears in the title sequence. The film was released on November 25, 1949, by United Artists.

[7] Kiss and Tell (1945 film)
Kiss and Tell is a 1945 American comedy film starring then 17-year-old Shirley Temple as Corliss Archer. In the film, two teenage girls cause their respective parents much concern when they start to become interested in boys. The parents' bickering about which girl is the worse influence causes more problems than it solves.

[8] Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs
The office of Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs was a British Government position, created in 2003. Certain functions of the Lord Chancellor which related to the Lord Chancellor's Department were transferred to the Secretary of State. At a later date further functions were also transferred to the Secretary of State for Constitutional Affairs from the First Secretary of State, a position within the government held by the Deputy Prime Minister.

[9] Village accountant
The Village Accountant (variously known as "Patwari", "Talati", "Patel", "Karnam", "Adhikari", "Shanbogaru","Patnaik" etc.) is an administrative government position found in rural parts of the Indian sub-continent. The office and the officeholder are called the "patwari" in Telangana, Bengal, North India and in Pakistan while in Sindh it is called "tapedar". The position is known as the "karnam" in Andhra Pradesh, "patnaik" in Orissa or "adhikari" in Tamil Nadu, while it is commonly known as the "talati" in Karnataka, Gujarat and Maharashtra. The position was known as the "kulkarni" in Northern Karnataka and Maharashtra. The position was known as the "shanbogaru" in South Karnataka.

[10] Charles Craft
Charles Craft (May 9, 1902 – September 19, 1968) was an English-born American film and television editor. Born in the county of Hampshire in England on May 9, 1902, Craft would enter the film industry in Hollywood in 1927. The first film he edited was the Universal Pictures silent film, "Painting the Town". Over the next 25 years, Craft would edit 90 feature-length films. In the early 1950s he would switch his focus to the small screen, his first show being "Racket Squad", from 1951–53, for which he was the main editor, editing 93 of the 98 episodes. He would work on several other series during the 1950s, including "Meet Corliss Archer" (1954), "Science Fiction Theatre" (1955–56), and "Highway Patrol" (1955–57). In the late 1950s and early 1960s he was one of the main editors on "Sea Hunt", starring Lloyd Bridges, editing over half of the episodes. His final film work would be editing "Flipper's New Adventure" (1964, the sequel to 1963's "Flipper". When the film was made into a television series, Craft would begin the editing duties on that show, editing the first 28 episodes before he retired in 1966. Craft died on September 19, 1968 in Los Angeles, California.
</task_context>

<deliverable>
Answer the question using only the provided context. Start with a short answer phrase, then briefly justify it.
</deliverable>

<evaluation_criteria>

</evaluation_criteria>
